sentence: Find a substitute material for PFAS material THV (elastomeric terpolymer of tetrafluoroethylene, hexafluoropropylene, and vinylidene fluoride) that has broad chemical resistance, including acids, bases, protic and aprotic solvents, oils, and salt solutions. Prioritize materials that have good flexibility (similar to THV), transparency, and low gas permeability.
keywords: elastomeric terpolymer of tetrafluoroethylene, hexafluoropropylene, and vinylidene fluoride
material_X: elastomeric terpolymer of tetrafluoroethylene, hexafluoropropylene, and vinylidene fluoride

required: Chemical resistance: strong acids, bases, protic & aprotic solvents, oils, saline solutions
Flexibility: room‑temperature stretch > 200 %, low modulus
Transparency: optical clarity, < 5 % haze
Low gas permeability: O₂ and H₂O vapor < X cm³/m²·day (benchmarking THV)
PFAS‑free composition (no perfluoro groups)
Low cross‑link density (≈ 1–2 %) to preserve elasticity
Elastomeric additive (5–15 wt %) for stretch‑to‑break & recovery
Nucleating agent (0.5–2 wt %) for fine crystalline lamellae
Tie‑resin/compatibilizer (1–3 wt % MAPP or equivalent) to reduce voids & creep
No high‑permeability plasticizers (use low‑molecular‑weight, immiscible additives only)
Mechanical performance: stretch‑to‑break > 200 %, recovery > 90 %, creep ≤ Y % (as per THV)
Processability: extrusion, injection molding, coating, scalable to commercial volumes
Cost‑efficiency: unit price comparable to or lower than PFAS THV
Regulatory compliance: meets REACH, EPA PFAS‑free criteria, no “fluorinated” labeling
Scalability: pilot‑scale data available, production‑scale feasibility confirmed
Critical THV traits to replicate:
Broad chemical resistance spectrum
Room‑temperature flexibility & recoverability
Optical transparency & low haze
Low gas permeability (barrier performance)
High stretch‑to‑break with minimal creep
Processable into tubing, seals, and film forms

extracted_keywords: Chemical resistance: strong acids, bases, protic & aprotic solvents, oils, saline solutions
Flexibility: room‑temperature stretch > 200 %, low modulus
Transparency: optical clarity, < 5 % haze
Low gas permeability: O₂ and H₂O vapor < X cm³/m²·day (benchmarking THV)
PFAS‑free composition (no perfluoro groups)
Low cross‑link density (≈ 1–2 %) to preserve elasticity
Elastomeric additive (5–15 wt %) for stretch‑to‑break & recovery
Nucleating agent (0.5–2 wt %) for fine crystalline lamellae
Tie‑resin/compatibilizer (1–3 wt % MAPP or equivalent) to reduce voids & creep
No high‑permeability plasticizers (use low‑molecular‑weight, immiscible additives only)
Mechanical performance: stretch‑to‑break > 200 %, recovery > 90 %, creep ≤ Y % (as per THV)
Processability: extrusion, injection molding, coating, scalable to commercial volumes
Cost‑efficiency: unit price comparable to or lower than PFAS THV
Regulatory compliance: meets REACH, EPA PFAS‑free criteria, no “fluorinated” labeling
Scalability: pilot‑scale data available, production‑scale feasibility confirmed
Critical THV traits to replicate:
Broad chemical resistance spectrum
Room‑temperature flexibility & recoverability
Optical transparency & low haze
Low gas permeability (barrier performance)
High stretch‑to‑break with minimal creep
Processable into tubing, seals, and film forms
extracted_constraints: Acid resistance – The material must withstand continuous exposure to at least 10 % (w/w) HCl at room temperature for 24 h without any measurable mass loss or loss of mechanical integrity.
Base resistance – The material must withstand continuous exposure to at least 10 % (w/w) NaOH at room temperature for 24 h without measurable mass loss or loss of mechanical integrity.
Solvent resistance – The material must resist both protic (e.g., ethanol) and aprotic (e.g., acetone) solvents at room temperature for 24 h, showing no measurable swelling or softening.
Oil resistance – The material must resist mineral oil (or comparable hydrocarbon oil) exposure at room temperature for 24 h without swelling, softening, or chemical degradation.
Salt‑solution resistance – The material must resist immersion in a 3.5 % (w/w) NaCl solution at room temperature for 24 h without measurable mass loss or loss of mechanical integrity.
Flexibility (elongation) – The material must exhibit an elongation‑to‑break of ≥ 200 % at 23 °C (i.e., comparable to THV’s typical flexibility).
Transparency – The material must have optical transmittance ≥ 80 % at 550 nm over a 1 mm thickness (i.e., be sufficiently clear for visual inspection and optical applications).
Gas‑permeability – The material must have an O₂ permeability ≤ 5 × 10⁻⁹ cm³(STP)·cm/(cm²·s·cmHg) (i.e., be a low‑permeability barrier suitable for food‑contact or pharmaceutical use).
PFAS‑free – The material must contain no per‑ or poly‑fluoroalkyl substances (including short‑chain fluorocarbon monomers, PFOS, PFOA, and any derivatives).
Hydrocarbon inertness – The material must not degrade (mass loss > 0.5 %) when exposed to n‑hexane or equivalent hydrocarbon for 24 h at room temperature.
Regulatory compliance – The material must meet FDA 21 CFR 170.1102 (or equivalent FDA medical‑device‑contact material) and REACH “polymers of low concern” requirements for non‑PFAS elastomers.
Temperature endurance – The material must sustain continuous service at ≥ 200 °C (i.e., retain its mechanical and barrier properties for 24 h at 200 °C).
